description: The provided image is unreadable and displays only a placeholder icon, so no visual scientific content can be directly verified. Based on the paper context and text extraction summary, this figure is inferred to correspond to the mechanical testing data for Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel. It likely presents engineering stress-strain curves, true stress-strain curves, and work hardening rate curves comparing the hot-rolled and quenched (HRQ) condition with samples intercritically annealed at 750°C for 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes. The extracted data indicate that the IA750_60 Min sample achieves the best combination of strength and ductility, with an ultimate tensile strength of approximately 658 MPa, total elongation of about 12%, and a peak product of strength and elongation (PSE) of roughly 7.9 GPa%. The work hardening rate reportedly exhibits two-stage behavior, with the IA60 condition showing the highest strain hardening capacity. These results support that the TRIP effect dominates the deformation behavior and that 60-minute intercritical annealing optimizes austenite stability and mechanical performance.

Figure 1 comprises three panels presenting the thermomechanical processing design, equilibrium phase predictions, and thermal dilation verification for an Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C (wt.%) low-density medium Mn steel. Panel (a) is a schematic process route showing: homogenization at 1100°C for 2 h, hot forging with air cooling, hot rolling with soaking at 1100°C for 2 h and two passes (50% reduction each, intermediate reheating at 1100°C for 5 min), water quenching, and intercritical annealing at 750°C for 5–180 min followed by air cooling. Panel (b) displays the Thermo-Calc equilibrium phase fraction versus temperature plot (TCFE9 database), indicating that BCC_A2 (δ-ferrite) persists from solidification to room temperature and coexists with FCC_A1 (austenite) in a duplex region from ~650°C to ~1350°C; this underpins the selection of 750°C for intercritical annealing. Panel (c) presents dilatometry heating-cooling curves for the hot-forged sample under 5 min and 15 min holding times, both nearly overlapping with only a slight deviation around ~600°C (marked by a black ellipse), which caption states reflects carbide/cementite dissolution rather than massive ferrite-to-austenite transformation, confirming the stability of δ-ferrite and minimal phase transformation during thermal cycling.

Figure 2 integrates X-ray diffraction, optical microscopy, SEM, and EBSD to compare the phase constitution and microstructure of as-cast and hot-forged specimens of the investigated high-Al low-density medium-Mn steel. Panel (a) shows XRD patterns indexed to α-ferrite (assumed δ-ferrite) and γ-austenite for both conditions; the caption reports Rietveld-derived austenite fractions of ~26 vol.% (cast) and ~29 vol.% (forged). Optical micrographs (b, c) of the cast specimen reveal a dual-phase microstructure containing dendritic austenite (blue arrows) within the δ-ferrite matrix. Panels (d, e) show the hot-forged specimen, where austenite appears largely elongated but still retains some dendritic remnants (blue arrows), suggesting incomplete dissolution during high-temperature soaking. The SEM image (f) and EBSD phase map superimposed on IQ (g) of the forged sample explicitly resolve the elongated austenite (γ) grains in the δ-ferrite matrix, with the EBSD legend confirming BCC (red) and FCC (green) phases. These results collectively establish that casting and hot forging both produce a stable δ-ferrite plus austenite dual-phase microstructure, and that thermomechanical processing slightly increases austenite fraction and modifies its morphology from dendritic to elongated.

Figure 3 presents a multi-scale microstructural characterization of the hot-rolled and quenched (HRQ) specimen in a high-Al (~5 wt%) low-density medium-Mn steel. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph (inset: differential interference contrast image) showing elongated grains along the rolling direction (RD), with visible δ-ferrite and α/γ regions; the scale bar is 100 μm. Panel (b) is a scanning electron micrograph (10 μm scale bar) revealing a δ-ferrite matrix containing α/γ martensite/austenite in lenticular morphology. Panel (c) provides EDS spectra and a quantitative composition table: the δ-ferrite phase contains 5.61±0.11 wt% Al and 7.02±0.25 wt% Mn, whereas the austenite/martensite phase contains 4.75±0.11 wt% Al and 8.36±0.16 wt% Mn, indicating Al enrichment in δ-ferrite and Mn enrichment in austenite/martensite. Panel (d) is an EBSD phase map superimposed on the IQ map, where red corresponds to BCC and green to FCC, supporting the dual-crystal-structure distribution. As the caption and text report, water quenching from ~800°C transformed part of the austenite into martensite, while δ-ferrite remained highly stable. Together, these observations establish the baseline three-phase microstructure (δ-ferrite, retained austenite, and α-martensite) and elemental partitioning in the HRQ condition.

Figure 4 presents scanning electron microscopy images and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy analyses for an Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel intercritically annealed at 750°C for 5 min (A1-A3), 30 min (B1-B3), 60 min (C1-C3), 120 min (D1-D3), and 180 min (E1-E3). Low-magnification micrographs (A1-E1) show the δ-ferrite matrix, untransformed austenite, and α/γ regions, while higher-magnification views (A2-E2) capture the progressive dissolution of prior martensite into needle-shaped and then globular reverted austenite embedded in intercritical ferrite. EDS spectra and quantification tables (A3-E3) reveal that δ-ferrite composition remains nearly unchanged (~5.3-5.9 wt.% Al, ~6.6-7.4 wt.% Mn), whereas untransformed austenite becomes enriched in Mn (up to 9.83±0.37 wt.% at 60 min) and reverted austenite becomes Mn-lean (down to 7.58±0.14 wt.% at 60 min) as annealing time increases. The microstructural evolution demonstrates that prolonged annealing drives reverted austenite to dissolve into intercritical ferrite via elemental partitioning, with nearly complete dissolution observed after 180 min, thereby supporting the correlation between austenite stability and the TRIP effect.

Figure 5 presents nanoindentation load-displacement curves (maximum load 30 mN) for δ-ferrite, martensite/reverted austenite, and untransformed austenite in an Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C steel, with optical micrographs inset showing indent locations marked by black triangles. The nine panels cover three processing conditions: hot-rolled & quenched (HRQ, A1–A3), intercritical annealing at 750 °C for 5 min (B1–B3), and 60 min (C1–C3). Visually, martensite in HRQ (A2) exhibits the shallowest penetration depth (~384 nm), whereas untransformed austenite (A3) reaches ~550 nm, indicating lower hardness. The accompanying text reports nano-hardness values of ~8897 MPa for martensite, ~4080 MPa for untransformed austenite, and ~4439 MPa for δ-ferrite in the HRQ specimen. After annealing, the martensite region transforms into reverted austenite with hardness decreasing from ~3867 MPa at 5 min to ~3478 MPa at 60 min, while δ-ferrite (~4267 MPa and ~4150 MPa) and untransformed austenite show little change with annealing time. These results confirm that intercritical annealing dissolves hard martensite into softer reverted austenite.

This figure presents the tensile response of Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel through engineering stress–strain curves (A1), true stress–strain curves (A2), and work-hardening rate (WHR) plots (B1–B4) for hot-rolled (HR) and specimens intercritically annealed at 750°C for 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 minutes. Panels A1 and A2 show that HR exhibits the highest strength but fractures at very low strain, whereas annealed samples display progressively improved ductility, with IA750_60 Min reaching the largest elongation before fracture. Panels B1–B4 each display two-stage WHR behavior: stage S1, where the WHR drops sharply attributed to δ-ferrite deformation, and stage S2, where WHR decreases more gradually due to simultaneous softening and TRIP effects. The accompanying text reports that the mean stage-S2 WHR rises from 1100–400 MPa at 5 min to 1700–1000 MPa at 30 min, peaks at 2000–1000 MPa for IA750_60 Min, and then falls to 1500–900 MPa at 180 min. The caption states that IA750_60 Min achieves optimum austenite stability, promoting the highest fraction of austenite-to-martensite transformation and thus the best combination of strength and ductility.

Figure 7 presents a multi-panel analysis linking phase constitution, austenite stability, and mechanical properties of a hot-rolled and quenched high (~5%) Al-added low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C) after intercritical annealing at 750°C for 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 min. Subfigures (a) and (b) show XRD patterns before and after tensile testing, respectively, indexing α-ferrite (δ/α) and γ-austenite peaks; γ reflections are strongest in IA750_5Min and progressively weaken with longer annealing. Subfigure (c) reports the austenite volume fraction (black bars before test, red bars after test), which peaks at ~46% for IA750_5Min and declines to ~30% at 120–180 min, alongside the transformation ratio (blue line, right axis) maximizing at ~20% for IA750_60Min and the mechanical stability parameter K (green line) showing a minimum at IA750_30Min. Subfigure (d) plots yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, total elongation, and the product of strength and elongation, demonstrating that IA750_60Min achieves the best synergy with UTS ~658 MPa and TE ~12%. Together, the data establish that annealing time governs the dissolution and elemental partitioning of reverted austenite, and the IA750_60Min condition yields optimum austenite mechanical stability and TRIP effect for enhanced strength-ductility balance.

This schematic figure illustrates the phase transformation sequence of martensite (α′) dissolution into reverted austenite (γ^rev) and intercritical ferrite (α) in a high (~5%) Al-added low-density medium Mn steel during intercritical annealing at 750°C, as indicated by the subfigure labels. Panel (a) shows the hot-rolled and quenched (HRQ) condition with martensite (α′) embedded in untransformed austenite (γ^o) within a delta ferrite (δ) matrix. Panels (b)–(f) depict progressive evolution at annealing times of 5, 30, 60, 120, and 180 min, respectively: needle-shaped γ^rev and α-ferrite form after 5 min; the needles progressively shorten and develop globular morphology by 60 min; and by 180 min only traces of γ^rev remain. Caption states that prolonged annealing causes Mn and Al partitioning from reverted austenite into adjacent untransformed austenite, reducing the fraction and stability of γ^rev. The figure supports the core conclusion that mechanical stability of reverted austenite and the resulting TRIP effect are determined by a balance among grain size, morphology, and solute enrichment that varies systematically with annealing duration, with optimum stability and enhanced TRIP reported at 60 min.

This figure presents high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis of the IA750_60 Min specimen from a Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C low-density medium-Mn steel, intercritically annealed at 750°C for 60 min. Panel (a) shows a bright-field TEM image revealing nanoscale microstructural features with varying diffraction contrast, while panel (b) provides a HAADF-STEM image where brighter regions correspond to higher average atomic number. Panels (c) through (g) display individual EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, Si, and C, respectively. The Mn map (d) exhibits pronounced enrichment in specific bright regions, whereas the Al map (e) shows corresponding depletion in those same areas. Si and C maps appear relatively uniform across the field. Panel (h) overlays all elemental distributions onto the HAADF image. As stated in the caption and text, the EDS maps confirm that reverted austenite is enriched in Mn and depleted in Al compared to intercritical ferrite (α-ferrite), verifying the dissolution of martensite into reverted austenite and intercritical ferrite during annealing. Critically, no precipitates or elemental clustering were detected, ruling out carbide formation in this condition.
